s_text:
**LLM-based Evaluation and Verification.** The use of large language models as evaluators has gained traction with the LLM-as-a-Judge paradigm [1], where strong models are used to assess the quality of model outputs in open-ended settings, achieving over 80% agreement with human ratings. This approach has been extended to multimodal settings through MLLM-as-a-Judge [2], where vision-language models are tasked with scoring, comparing, and ranking multimodal outputs. However, LLM-based judges exhibit systematic biases including positional effects, phrasing sensitivity, and self-preference [1, 2], motivating bias-mitigation strategies such as criteria-order randomization and label rephrasing. Beyond evaluation, generative verifiers [3] reframe reward modeling as next-token prediction, enabling chain-of-thought reasoning [4] during verification, while self-consistency via majority voting [5] aggregates diverse reasoning paths to improve reliability at test time. Despite these advances, existing work has focused primarily on static text or multimodal outputs, and the use of MLLMs as verifiers of interactive agent behavior—where trajectories span multiple steps and modalities—remains comparatively underexplored. This motivates the target paper's systematic investigation of MLLM-based verifiers across diverse interactive environments and its identification of agreement bias as a critical failure mode.

**Autonomous Agents and Self-Improvement.** Language models have increasingly been deployed as agents that interleave reasoning and action in interactive environments. The ReAct paradigm [6] demonstrates that generating reasoning traces alongside actions improves grounding and interpretability in tasks such as web navigation and embodied interaction. Building on such architectures, Reflexion [7] introduces verbal reinforcement learning, where agents produce reflective textual summaries from environmental feedback and store them in episodic memory to guide subsequent attempts without model fine-tuning. These self-improvement pipelines critically depend on verifier quality: when a verifier produces false positives, agents receive no corrective signal for flawed behavior, limiting their capacity for self-correction. Similarly, in robotic manipulation, Diffusion Policy [8] learns visuomotor policies from demonstration data through conditional denoising diffusion, and its deployment in open-ended tasks would benefit from reliable verification of task completion. The challenge of obtaining scalable, accurate verification thus directly constrains the effectiveness of agent learning paradigms.

**Benchmarks for Multimodal Agent Evaluation.** Studying MLLM verifiers requires environments with diverse tasks and nuanced success criteria. WebArena [9] established a realistic self-hosted web environment spanning e-commerce, social forums, and content management, evaluating agents by functional correctness. VisualWebArena [10] extended this to multimodal settings with 910 visually grounded tasks and introduced Set-of-Marks prompting to simplify visual action grounding in web interfaces. OSWorld [11] further broadened the scope to real computer desktop environments with 369 tasks involving multi-application workflows across Ubuntu, Windows, and macOS. In robotics, the robomimic benchmark [12] provides long-horizon manipulation tasks and expert demonstration datasets, with tasks such as tool hang requiring multi-stage reasoning over physical interactions. Collectively, these benchmarks span web, desktop, and robotic domains, enabling evaluation of MLLM verifiers across diverse modalities and task complexities. However, their oracle evaluation scripts—typically hard-coded rules applied to subsets of environment states—can be lenient or brittle, motivating the target paper's investigation into MLLMs as more flexible alternatives for open-ended verification.

[2] Dongping Chen, Ruoxi Chen, Shilin Zhang, Yaochen Wang, Yinuo Liu et al. "MLLM-as-a-Judge: Assessing Multimodal LLM-as-a-Judge with Vision-Language Benchmark." arXiv:2402.04788, 2024.

[3] Lunjun Zhang, Arian Hosseini, Hritik Bansal, Mehran Kazemi, Aviral Kumar et al. "Generative Verifiers: Reward Modeling as Next-Token Prediction." arXiv:2408.15240, 2024.

[4] Jason Wei, Xuezhi Wang, Dale Schuurmans, Maarten Bosma, Brian Ichter, Fei Xia, Ed H. Chi, Quoc V. Le, Denny Zhou. "Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models." arXiv:2201.11903, 2022.

[5] Xuezhi Wang, Jason Wei, Dale Schuurmans, Quoc V. Le, Ed H. Chi, Sharan Narang, Aakanksha Chowdhery, Denny Zhou. "Self-Consistency Improves Chain of Thought Reasoning in Language Models." arXiv:2203.11171, 2022.

[6] Shunyu Yao, Jeffrey Zhao, Dian Yu, Nan Du, Izhak Shafran, Karthik Narasimhan, Yuan Cao. "ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models." arXiv:2210.03629, 2022.

[7] Noah Shinn, Federico Cassano, Ashwin Gopinath, Karthik Narasimhan, Shunyu Yao. "Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning." arXiv:2303.11366, 2023.

[8] Cheng Chi, Siyuan Feng, Yilun Du, Zhenjia Xu, Eric Cousineau, Benjamin Burchfiel, Shuran Song. "Diffusion Policy: Visuomotor Policy Learning via Action Diffusion." arXiv:2303.04137, 2023.

[9] Shuyan Zhou, Frank F. Xu, Hao Zhu, Xuhui Zhou, Robert Lo, Abishek Sridhar, Xianyi Cheng, Yonatan Bisk, Daniel Fried, Uri Alon, Graham Neubig. "WebArena: A Realistic Web Environment for Building Autonomous Agents." arXiv:2307.13854, 2023.

[10] Jing Yu Koh, Robert Lo, Lawrence Jang, Vikram Duvvur, Ming Chong Lim, Po-Yu Huang, Graham Neubig, Shuyan Zhou, Ruslan Salakhutdinov, Daniel Fried. "VisualWebArena: Evaluating Multimodal Agents on Realistic Visual Web Tasks." arXiv:2401.13649, 2024.

[11] Tianbao Xie, Danyang Zhang, Jixuan Chen, Xiaochuan Li, Siheng Zhao et al. "OSWorld: Benchmarking Multimodal Agents for Open-Ended Tasks in Real Computer Environments." arXiv:2404.07972, 2024.

[12] Ajay Mandlekar, Danfei Xu, Josiah Wong, Soroush Nasiriany, Chen Wang et al. "What Matters in Learning from Offline Human Demonstrations for Robot Manipulation." arXiv:2108.03298, 2021.
g_text:
MLLMs as Evaluators.
(M)LLMs have been employed as evaluators of model outputs in various scenarios under various names---(M)LLMs as judges, critics, reward models, value functions.
This work focuses on multimodal and environment-interaction scenarios.
MLLMs have been used to score and filter agent trajectories for subsequent use in finetuning~, and test-time refinements such as to induce prompts, reflections, and tools~.
They have also served as a source of real-time feedback by producing natural language ``critiques''~, scores to rank action proposals in search~, and rewards for training~.

AI Agents.
There is growing interest in building AI agents to act in various environments, including the web~, mobile phones~, and computers~.
In this field, the work most closely related to ours is~, which uses a GPT-4V-based evaluator, prompted with benchmark-specific rubrics, to evaluate trajectories for Reflexion~ and behavior cloning.
Following works employ a similar evaluator to guide tree search~, filter trajectories to generate text-based memories or tools~ to boost agent performance in (Visual)WebArena~, and for RL training in simpler environments~.

Test-time scaling. 
(TTS) is a major paradigm to improve model performance without increasing parameters~.
Early work~ shows that prompting LLMs to generate ``chains-of-thought'' yields substantial gains in reasoning-oriented tasks.
This idea has been extended to several settings, including multimodal~ and environment-interaction~.
Orthogonal approaches scale test-time compute via sampling and search~, where multiple generations are selected through heuristics~ or verifiers~.
Recent work leverages sampling, RL, and formal verifiers to train (M)LLMs that autonomously generate reasoning traces~.
Extending these methods to open-ended problems requires flexible and multimodal verification, for which MLLMs offer an appealing solution.
